context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Geographic and Demographic Data

Recommended due date: June 13 by 11:59 pm EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content, but no direct instructor feedback will be offered this week. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners for a peer learning opportunity.

Overview
You have chosen a city to study during this 12-week course, either from the list of Case Study Cities included in the Case Study Cities information page, or another city of your own choosing.

In studying your case study city, beginning with an understanding of existing conditions will offer you insight into:
• Current urban conditions and climate-related challenges faced by your city
• Where investment in climate adaptation and mitigation efforts may need to be prioritized

Assignment Tasks
You will complete a Geographic and Demographic Data Table for your chosen city. Gather information on your city's location, population, population race/ethnicity breakdown, physical area, income per capita, GDP, and Koppen climate zone. Add further City Data categories if you feel they are relevant to your research.

Resources
See the example below of a completed Geographic and Demographic Data Table.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i'm kinda lost on the city case study table tbh, like idk what counts as the main geographic vs demographic data.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point to think about! Let me ask you this: if you had to describe a city to someone who had never heard of it, what kinds of facts would you share about the *place itself* versus the *people living there*? What comes to mind?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing general confusion about the distinction between geographic and demographic data. Rather than explaining the difference directly, I should use a Socratic question to help them discover the distinction themselves. I'll keep it bite-sized and let them reason through it.

exchanges.student: umm idk, like place = where it is maybe? but then climate and area and population all blur together for me rn.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable start. Let's try one: population count — does that tell you something about the *place*, or about the *people in it*? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making some progress by identifying 'where it is' as geographic, but is struggling to sort the other categories. Rather than explaining the taxonomy, I should push them to think about each category one at a time. I'll ask a targeted question about one of the blurry examples to help them build the distinction themselves.

exchanges.student: i guess people? but then the assignment puts everything in one table so now i'm confused again lol.
exchanges.tutor: Fair observation — the table combines everything, so the geo vs. demographic distinction matters less than you think. Have you looked at the specific data categories the assignment actually asks you to collect? What's on that list?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is getting distracted by the table format rather than focusing on the content. I should acknowledge their observation briefly and redirect them to what actually matters for the assignment: gathering the specific data categories listed, not perfectly categorizing them as geo vs demographic. The assignment lists specific fields, so I should point them there.

exchanges.student: yeah but it's a lot all at once tbh: location, population, race/ethnicity, area, income per capita, gdp, koppen zone... i can't tell what each one is supposed to show.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid list to work from though — you've already got it. Let's not tackle all of them at once. Pick one from that list that feels most straightforward to you. What would that data point tell you about your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is feeling overwhelmed by the list. Rather than explaining each category, I should help them take it one at a time and activate their prior knowledge. I'll pick the most concrete/accessible one to start and ask them what they think it reveals about their city. This uses guided discovery and keeps things bite-sized.

exchanges.student: maybe location? but idk what i'm supposed to say besides like the country and region, and that feels too basic fr.
exchanges.tutor: Think about the course theme for a second — this is a class about climate and cities. Why might a city's location matter in that context? What could location tell you that's relevant beyond just 'it's in X country'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is undervaluing what location data can reveal. Rather than explaining it, I should push them to think about why location might matter for a course specifically about climate action and urban sustainability. This will help them see the relevance themselves.

exchanges.student: maybe like coast/inland or north/south? idk, can u just explain what each category is for bc i'm mixing all of it up.
exchanges.tutor: You just answered your own question — coast vs. inland, latitude — those are exactly the kinds of details that make location meaningful for climate research. You don't need me to explain each category; you're already doing the right thinking. Try applying that same logic to one more category from the list. What might, say, the Koppen climate zone tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is actually making good progress - they just identified two really relevant geographic factors (coast/inland, north/south) without realizing it. I should affirm this and redirect rather than explain all categories. They're asking me to do the work for them, which I should decline. I'll point out that they're already reasoning well and push them to apply the same thinking to the next category.

exchanges.student: ngl i still don't get the overall point, like how do all these random facts connect in the assignment?
exchanges.tutor: That's worth figuring out. The assignment overview actually addresses this directly — what does it say about why you're gathering this data? Take another look and tell me what you find.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stepping back and asking about the big picture, which is actually a good sign of metacognitive awareness. However, the assignment overview explicitly states the purpose, so I should redirect them there rather than explaining it myself. I'll ask them to look at the assignment overview and reflect on what it says.

exchanges.student: it says smth about understanding existing conditions and climate challenges, but that's still super vague to me tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you found it. Now make it concrete: what city did you choose? And can you think of one climate challenge that city actually faces? That might help the purpose click.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student found the right passage but finds it vague. I should help them make it concrete by connecting it to their specific city rather than explaining abstractly. A targeted question about their chosen city will ground the abstract purpose in something real.

exchanges.student: i picked jakarta, but then that makes it worse bc i know it has flooding and stuff and now every category feels important lol.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a great sign — feeling like every category matters means you're starting to see the point of the exercise. So let's put that to work: can you start filling in even just two or three categories for Jakarta in your table? What do you already know or can quickly look up?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a great connection - they know Jakarta faces flooding and are starting to see relevance in the data categories. This is genuine progress. I should affirm this and help them see that feeling like 'every category is important' is actually the point, not a problem. I can nudge them to start filling in the table using Jakarta as the lens.

exchanges.student: i can prob look stuff up, i'm just stuck on what each number is *doing* in the table tbh.
exchanges.tutor: We've been circling this question a few times now. I think the best way to make it click is to actually start filling in the table — thinking about it abstractly isn't moving you forward. Pick Jakarta, look up two categories, plug them in, and then tell me what you found. The purpose will become clearer once you're working with real data.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles about the purpose of the data categories. We've now touched on this same concern multiple times. Per the rules, I should notice this redundancy and push the student to start writing/integrating what we've discussed rather than continuing to talk abstractly. It's time to redirect to actually starting the table.
